revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery == <gallery> File:Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), koshitsu-3.jpg| Mukoyama No.1 (Iwaya Kofun) sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-2, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-3, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.4 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, entrance.jpg|Mukoyama No.5 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg| Mukoyama No.5 sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-6, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.6 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-7, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.7 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-8, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.8 File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg| Ishiumadani Kofun File:石馬谷古墳出土 石馬 (複製).JPG|Stone horse (ICP) from Ishiumadani Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commonscat|Mukoyama Kofun Group}} *[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/14198.htm Yonago City home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/147A9AFDF45291F34925796F0007FCF3?OpenDocument Tottori Prefecture home page] {{in lang|ja}} [[Category:History of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Yonago, Tottori]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery == <gallery> File:Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), koshitsu-3.jpg| Mukoyama No.1 (Iwaya Kofun) sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-2, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-3, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.4 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, entrance.jpg|Mukoyama No.5 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg| Mukoyama No.5 sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-6, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.6 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-7, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.7 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-8, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.8 File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg| Ishiumadani Kofun File:石馬谷古墳出土 石馬 (複製).JPG|Stone horse (ICP) from Ishiumadani Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commonscat|Mukoyama Kofun Group}} *[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/14198.htm Yonago City home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/147A9AFDF45291F34925796F0007FCF3?OpenDocument Tottori Prefecture home page] {{in lang|ja}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::History of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::Yonago, Tottori]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::Kofun clusters]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:::dd]][[en:Mukoeyama Kofun Cluster]]

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery == <gallery> File:Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), koshitsu-3.jpg| Mukoyama No.1 (Iwaya Kofun) sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-2, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-3, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.4 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, entrance.jpg|Mukoyama No.5 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg| Mukoyama No.5 sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-6, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.6 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-7, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.7 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-8, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.8 File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg| Ishiumadani Kofun File:石馬谷古墳出土 石馬 (複製).JPG|Stone horse (ICP) from Ishiumadani Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mounds]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of burial mounds were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square burial mounds. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery == <gallery> File:Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), koshitsu-3.jpg| Mukoyama No.1 (Iwaya Kofun) sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-2, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-3, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.4 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, entrance.jpg|Mukoyama No.5 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg| Mukoyama No.5 sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-6, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.6 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-7, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.7 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-8, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.8 File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg| Ishiumadani Kofun File:石馬谷古墳出土 石馬 (複製).JPG|Stone horse (ICP) from Ishiumadani Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]]

It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | native_name =向山古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Iwaya Kofun entrance | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukoyama Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = Kofun cluster | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|16.4|N|133|26|25.9|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[San'in region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[Yodoe Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery == <gallery> File:Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), koshitsu-3.jpg| Mukoyama No.1 (Iwaya Kofun) sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-2, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-3, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.4 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, entrance.jpg|Mukoyama No.5 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg| Mukoyama No.5 sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-6, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.6 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-7, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.7 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-8, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.8 File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg| Ishiumadani Kofun File:石馬谷古墳出土 石馬 (複製).JPG|Stone horse (ICP) from Ishiumadani Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[:en:Hoki Province|Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Yodoe Station|Yodoe Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Mukoyama Kofun cluster'''|向山古墳群| Mukoyama Kofun-gun}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofuns]] located in the Yodoe neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]], [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932, with the area under protection expanded in 1999.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2110|title=向山古墳群|trans-title= Mukoyama Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== During the Kofun period, the Yodoe Plain formed a lagoon, and hundreds of Kofuns were constructed in the surrounding hills. The Mukaiyama cluster is located to the east of the lagoon, and consists of a 17 kofun, including nine keyhole-shaped, five circular, and two square Kofuns. Within this group, the Iwaya Kofun (Mukoyama No.1) is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a length of 54 meters, height of six meters and a stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. Its design shows an influence of the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo region]]. In addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' and figurative ''haniwa'' (warrior dolls, horse-shaped ''haniwa'', waterfowl-shaped ''haniwa'', etc.) have been found. [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included Iron swords and gilt-bronze harnesses. The tumulus is estimated to have been constructed in the latter half of the 6th century in the late Kofun period. It was designated a National Historic Site in 1932.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 1985 to 1989, an extensive surveying and [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted, and the area of the National Historic Site designation expanded in 1999. Among the ''kofun'' constructed from the latter half of the 5th century to the latter half of the 6th century, Mukaiyama No. 3, Mukaiyama No. 4, Ishiumadani, and Iwaya Kofun were typical ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' of western [[:en:Hoki Province|Hoki Province]]. A stone horse found in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] at the Ishiumatani Kofun suggest exchanges with northern [[Kyushu]], and the only other known examples are from that region.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It was made the ''[[shintai]]'' of a nearby [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and was designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1959.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9815|title=石馬|trans-title= Sekiba|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
Currently, the site is open to the public as {{nihongo|Hōki Kodai no Oka Park|伯耆古代の丘公園}}, located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Yodoe Station|Yodoe Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery == <gallery> File:Iwaya Kofun (Yonago), koshitsu-3.jpg| Mukoyama No.1 (Iwaya Kofun) sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-2, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-3, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.3 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.4 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, entrance.jpg|Mukoyama No.5 File:Chojaganaru Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg| Mukoyama No.5 sarcophagus File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-6, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.6 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-7, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.7 File:Mukoyama Kofun Group-8, funkyu.jpg| Mukoyama No.8 File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg| {{ill|Ishiumadani Kofun|ja|%E7%9F%B3%E9%A6%AC%E8%B0%B7%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiumadani Kofun|qq=}}|alt=Ishiumadani Kofun File:石馬谷古墳出土 石馬 (複製).JPG|Stone horse (ICP) from Ishiumadani Kofun </gallery>
